followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I lowered the overall score and verdict from weak_evidence to major_flaw. The other reviews strengthened two concerns: first, the core three-mode Hamiltonian itself may be spurious in Fermi-normal coordinates; second, thermal/mechanical noise and the SQUID transduction chain are missing from the feasibility argument. I still do not adopt the most categorical fatal judgment without PDF-level algebraic spot-checking, but the unresolved Hamiltonian and dimensional-rate issues are severe enough to reduce technical soundness.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: I do not accept as established that the three-mode interaction is definitely absent solely from the review text; this requires direct PDF algebra and gauge checking. I treat it as a major unresolved objection rather than a certified fatal error.

One review states stimulated emission uses n~10^6 photons, while the paper text in the bundle refers to much larger occupations in the numerical discussion. The exact number should be checked from the PDF, so I avoid relying on that specific value.

The long-wavelength approximation objection is comparatively weak as stated: for kHz gravitational waves and meter-scale bars it is normally well satisfied, though the paper should specify its target band.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The thermal phonon/noise objection is valid and was underemphasized in my prior review; the paper starts from a clean initial state without specifying cooling or background rejection.
The discrete-system rate objection is valid and aligns with my prior concern about squared delta functions and finite-time resonance.
The coherent-array scaling objection is valid; no phase-coherent collective model is given.
The SQUID readout objection is valid as an incompleteness of the experimental proposal.
The factual GW150914 error is valid but minor.
The possible dimensional inconsistency of the final rate is a serious point, though it needs equation-level verification because the supplied HTML omits some formula content.

main_claims.claim: A charged resonant bar in a cavity has an effective Hamiltonian containing detector-GW, detector-photon, photon-GW, and detector-photon-GW interaction terms.
main_claims.notes: The general reduced-model strategy is plausible, but the paper bundle does not render the equations fully and the claimed mixed GW-photon-phonon term is not derived in enough detail to audit. I am not prepared to accept the stronger claim by another reviewer that it is definitely spurious without checking the PDF algebra, but the objection is serious enough to lower technical confidence.
main_claims.paper_location: Sec. II.A, Eq. (8)-Eq. (10) and surrounding discussion

main_claims.claim: The three-mode term enables photon emission with simultaneous mechanical excitation at the resonance omega = omega_0 + Omega_P.
main_claims.notes: Given the displayed verbal description of the interaction Hamiltonian, the selection-rule story is locally plausible: a GW time dependence can supply the energy mismatch while photon number increases and the mechanical oscillator is excited. But this inherits the unresolved Hamiltonian/gauge/normalization issue and the rotating-wave selection is not justified quantitatively.
main_claims.paper_location: End of Sec. II.A; Sec. III.A, Eq. (11)-Eq. (13)

main_claims.claim: The transition probability/rate scales as n_Pi+1, giving stimulated-emission enhancement.
main_claims.notes: The Bose enhancement factor is a standard consequence of a creation operator matrix element, so this part is plausible conditional on the interaction. The conversion from finite-time probability to an observable constant rate is not soundly presented for a discrete cavity-mechanical system.
main_claims.paper_location: Sec. III.A, Eq. (13)-Eq. (14) and paragraph after Eq. (14)

main_claims.claim: The numerical benchmark with q = 1 C, L = 1 m, m_0 = 10^-6 kg, Omega_P = 100 Hz, and large photon occupation yields experimentally detectable stimulated emission.
main_claims.notes: The benchmark rests on unvalidated charge, cavity-mode, photon-lifetime, SI-normalization, and noise assumptions. The other reviewers reinforced that the thermal/mechanical background and readout chain are not analyzed.
main_claims.paper_location: Sec. III.A numerical paragraph after Eq. (14)

main_claims.claim: A coherent array of oscillators gives large, possibly N^2, amplification and makes a tabletop detector feasible.
main_claims.notes: The paper asserts collective amplification without a collective Hamiltonian, phase-matching condition, decoherence model, or noise floor. This remains one of the main gaps.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The claimed three-mode term follows from a gauge-consistent reduction of the Fermi-normal-coordinate particle action plus Maxwell action.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the mixed h A xi or h A pi_xi coupling is absent, gauge removable, or differently normalized, the main photon-emission mechanism collapses.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A physically realizable high-Q low-frequency EM mode can be described by the single-mode cavity normalization used in Eq. (7)-Eq. (14).
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. III.A numerical estimate and Sec. III.B implementation
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The detectability estimate uses Omega_P = 100 Hz, L = 1 m, and huge photon occupation. Without a real mode architecture and loss model, the stimulated-emission calculation is not experimentally grounded.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A microgram-scale or crystal resonator can maintain the effective net charge used in the benchmark without breakdown, leakage, or dominant environmental coupling.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. II paragraph before Eq. (3); Sec. III.A numerical estimate
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The signal rate scales quadratically with charge, so realistic charge limits could reduce the rate by many orders of magnitude.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The oscillator can be prepared sufficiently close to the mechanical state assumed in the transition calculation, or thermal backgrounds can be subtracted below the GW-induced signal.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. III.A transition setup; Sec. III.B implementation
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: At low mechanical frequencies the thermal phonon occupation is large at ordinary temperatures, and the paper does not specify cooling or a noise model.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The coherent array really adds amplitudes rather than merely rates.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The proposed tabletop feasibility relies on large collective enhancement; incoherent addition would greatly weaken the final rate.

missing_assumptions.assumption: A finite-time perturbative calculation can be converted into a stable observable rate for the proposed discrete system.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: At exact resonance the paper's rate extraction grows with observation time. A consistent treatment needs damping, continuum density of states, or coherent Rabi dynamics.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Eq. (8)-Eq. (10) in the PDF actually produce a gauge-consistent GW-photon-phonon coupling term, or is the claimed term an artifact of the reduction or notation?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The supplied HTML omits key equation bodies, and the issue depends on detailed Fermi-normal-coordinate expansion, minimal coupling, and gauge conventions.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the SI-restored transition probability/rate around Eq. (14) dimensionally correct, including the photon-mode normalization and the units carried by the finite-time delta-function replacement?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The formula is central but not fully rendered in the bundle; a line-by-line dimensional audit of the PDF is needed.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Can the proposed low-frequency high-Q EM mode be realized with the stated effective mode volume and photon occupation, or must it be modeled as a circuit mode rather than a 1 m cavity photon mode?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires experimental cavity/circuit electrodynamics expertise and details not supplied by the paper.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What realistic effective charge can be carried by the proposed resonators under cryogenic high-Q conditions?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The answer depends on material, geometry, breakdown, screening, charge noise, and environmental coupling not modeled in the manuscript.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Under a realistic damping/noise model, does the signal-to-noise ratio remain detectable after thermal phonons, pump leakage, cavity losses, and SQUID/readout noise are included?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper contains no quantitative noise budget or transduction model, so a specialist calculation is required.

novelty_assessment: The proposal is moderately novel as a synthesis of charged resonant-bar mechanics, cavity optomechanics, and semiclassical Gertsenshtein-style conversion. The other reviews did not materially change my novelty view: the conceptual combination is interesting, but the paper does not compare quantitatively to existing transducer, Weber-bar, or photon-graviton conversion proposals, and the novelty is undermined by unresolved derivational and feasibility issues.
score_rationale: The paper has an intriguing mechanism, but the main conclusion rests on an inadequately derived three-mode interaction and a rate calculation that is not validly connected to a discrete, noisy experimental system. Feasibility depends on extreme charge, questionable low-frequency cavity assumptions, unsupported coherent-array enhancement, and missing thermal/readout noise analysis. Because the core effect may still be salvageable after a careful derivation, I do not score it as certainly wrong, but in its present form it has major unresolved flaws.
summary: The paper proposes a charged Weber-bar/cavity system in which a classical gravitational wave mediates a three-mode interaction between a mechanical oscillator and a photon mode, producing photon emission at a resonance. After considering the other reviews, I still view the work as an interesting but not yet credible detection proposal. I do not certify the full derivation. The strongest unresolved issues are the existence and normalization of the claimed three-mode Hamiltonian, the use of a discrete-mode perturbative transition probability as a constant rate, and the physical feasibility assumptions behind the numerical detectability claims.

technical_objections.evidence: Another reviewer argued that the Fermi-normal-coordinate particle action gives tidal terms quadratic in separation and ordinary minimal coupling q dot{x}.A, not a linear h dot{x} A term. From the bundle, the equations are incompletely rendered, so I cannot verify the exact algebra. However, the paper itself gives only a compressed transition from the action/mode expansion to Eq. (10), and all later emission claims depend on this term.
technical_objections.objection: The derivation of the claimed three-mode GW-photon-phonon Hamiltonian is insufficiently established and may contain a spurious metric-dependent minimal-coupling term.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. II.A, Eq. (8)-Eq. (10), especially the final interaction term discussed after Eq. (10)

technical_objections.evidence: The paper uses a squared resonance delta function, replaces the delta by a finite observation-time factor, and then defines Gamma = P/tau. At exact resonance this leaves a rate depending on the observation time, which is characteristic of coherent finite-time growth rather than a constant decay rate. For a single cavity photon mode and single oscillator mode one expects Rabi/finite-time dynamics unless a continuum or damping bath is explicitly included.
technical_objections.objection: The finite-time transition-rate derivation treats a discrete cavity-mechanical transition as if it produced a time-independent irreversible rate.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.A, Eq. (13)-Eq. (14) and following paragraph

technical_objections.evidence: My prior concern was that the EM-mode normalization and conversion from dimensionless variables to SI units are unclear. Another reviewer asserted the final rate has units of s^-3 rather than s^-1. I cannot fully confirm that from the rendered bundle, but because Eq. (14) is the basis for all numerical estimates, the missing normalization and delta-function units are a serious technical vulnerability.
technical_objections.objection: The dimensional restoration and numerical rate formula are not auditable and may be dimensionally inconsistent.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. II.A Eq. (7)-Eq. (10); Sec. III.A Eq. (14) and the unnumbered rate after it

technical_objections.evidence: The paper models a single EM cavity mode with Omega_P = 100 Hz and L = 1 m. A free-space 100 Hz electromagnetic wavelength is of order millions of meters, and the manuscript does not explain an effective lumped-element/circuit mode, mode volume, shielding, quality factor, or coupling normalization that would make the cavity-QED treatment valid.
technical_objections.objection: The assumed 100 Hz electromagnetic photon mode in a meter-scale high-Q cavity is not physically justified.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.A numerical benchmark and Sec. III.B implementation discussion

technical_objections.evidence: The claimed signal rate scales as q^2. A one-coulomb net charge on a microgram oscillator would imply extreme electrostatic fields, self-energy, discharge risk, and environmental coupling; none are modeled. Reducing the feasible charge would directly reduce the claimed rate.
technical_objections.objection: The use of q = 1 C on an effective m_0 = 10^-6 kg oscillator is a critical unexplained idealization.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.A numerical benchmark after Eq. (14)

technical_objections.evidence: The calculation starts from a mechanical ground state and specified photon occupation, while Sec. III.B does not discuss cryogenic operation, sideband cooling, mechanical damping, thermal phonons, cavity leakage, pump leakage, Johnson noise, or radiation from ordinary charged-oscillator coupling. At kHz mechanical frequencies, thermal occupation is enormous unless the device is cooled and actively controlled.
technical_objections.objection: Thermal and ordinary electromagnetic backgrounds are not controlled.

technical_objections.evidence: Independent oscillators normally add rates unless a specified collective mode makes emissions phase coherent and indistinguishable. The paper gives no collective Hamiltonian, phase-locking mechanism, phase-matching condition, spatial coherence analysis, or decoherence estimate.
technical_objections.objection: The N^2 coherent-array enhancement is asserted rather than derived.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. III.B, discussion of arrays and amplification

technical_objections.evidence: The text says unphysical processes are dropped, but the dropped terms and approximation regime are not specified. Since the resonance conditions and rate estimate depend on which terms survive, the validity of this reduction should be stated explicitly.
technical_objections.objection: The rotating-wave or term-dropping step is not justified.

technical_objections.evidence: The manuscript says emitted photons are converted into a measurable DC signal using a SQUID, but a SQUID measures magnetic flux/current through a circuit, not abstract cavity photon number. The antenna or circuit transduction chain, coupling efficiency, bandwidth, and noise temperature are missing.
technical_objections.objection: The proposed SQUID readout is underspecified.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper describes the first detected gravitational-wave signal as coming from two inspiralling neutron stars, but GW150914 was a binary black-hole merger. This does not affect the calculation, but it is a presentation and care issue.
technical_objections.objection: There is a factual error in the introduction about the first LIGO detection source.
